description: What threat intelligence interviews test: the ability to collect, analyze, and operationalize intelligence about threats. Tests framework fluency (ATT&CK, Diamond Model, Kill Chain), analytical rigor (distinguishing fact from inference), and operational integration (how intelligence actually changes defensive posture).

junior.label: Junior / Associate CTI Analyst
characteristics: Understands threat intelligence as a concept but cannot distinguish between raw data, information, and finished intelligence
Knows IOCs exist (IPs, domains, hashes) but uses them interchangeably without understanding their decay rates
Familiar with MITRE ATT&CK as a reference but cannot map an observed behavior to a specific technique ID
Cannot distinguish between strategic, operational, tactical, and technical intelligence
Describes threat intelligence as 'monitoring for bad IPs and domains'
Cannot explain the intelligence cycle: planning, collection, processing, analysis, dissemination, feedback
Has used threat intelligence platforms for IOC lookup but not for threat actor profiling
Cannot explain the difference between a threat actor, a threat group, and a campaign
Has heard of STIX/TAXII but cannot explain what they are or how they're used
Cannot explain why a VirusTotal hit is NOT sufficient intelligence
Does not understand the concept of intelligence confidence levels or uncertainty communication
Confuses OSINT with cyber threat intelligence
Cannot explain what TLP (Traffic Light Protocol) is used for in intelligence sharing
Has no understanding of adversary attribution and its difficulty
Thinks all threat intelligence is about technical indicators
example_phrases: I monitor OSINT feeds for IOCs and block them in our firewalls
I use VirusTotal and other tools to check if something is malicious
I've used MITRE ATT&CK to look up techniques
I subscribe to threat intel feeds and import the indicators
I look at threat reports from vendors to understand what attackers are doing
We block IPs from threat intelligence feeds at the perimeter
I know APT groups are sophisticated attackers from nation states
red_flags: Claims CTI experience but cannot explain the difference between tactical and strategic intelligence
Thinks threat intelligence is the same as collecting IOCs from feeds
Cannot explain TLP classifications or why they exist
Does not understand why an IP address becomes useless as an IOC within days
Thinks MITRE ATT&CK attribution means identifying which country launched an attack
Cannot explain what a STIX object represents
Has no framework for assessing intelligence source reliability
expected_frameworks: MITRE ATT&CK (awareness)
Basic Cyber Kill Chain awareness
TLP classifications

mid.label: Mid-Level CTI Analyst
characteristics: Understands the intelligence cycle and can apply it to a real intelligence requirement
Distinguishes between tactical (IOCs), operational (TTPs, campaign tracking), and strategic (threat actor motivations, targeting) intelligence
Understands IOC decay: IP addresses decay in hours, domains in days, tool hashes in weeks, TTPs decay slowly
Can apply the Diamond Model to analyze an intrusion: adversary, capability, infrastructure, victim
Can map observed behaviors to specific MITRE ATT&CK techniques and sub-techniques
Has produced a finished intelligence report with confidence assessments and source citations
Understands how to structure STIX 2.1 objects: threat actors, malware, attack patterns, indicators
Can pivot on infrastructure: IP -> ASN -> hosting provider -> co-hosted domains -> related campaigns
Knows common threat actor categories: financially motivated (FIN groups), nation-state (APT groups), hacktivists
Understands attribution challenges and can explain why definitive attribution is rare
Has experience consuming and contributing to threat intelligence platforms (MISP, OpenCTI)
Can write intelligence requirements (PIRs/IRs) that are specific and answerable
Knows common malware families and their associated threat actors: Emotet (TA542), Cobalt Strike (used by multiple actors)
Has performed OSINT investigations using Shodan, Censys, RiskIQ/PassiveTotal, Maltego
Understands the difference between technical indicators and behavioral indicators in terms of detection durability
example_phrases: The Diamond Model helped me connect what initially looked like two separate incidents - same infrastructure ASN and a TTP overlap with previously attributed APT29 activity
This IP is going to be useless in 48 hours - it's rotating C2 infrastructure. The durable intelligence is the user agent string and the beacon interval pattern, which we mapped to T1071.001

I wrote a Priority Intelligence Requirement asking specifically about threat actors targeting financial services firms that use a specific legacy software stack - that's answerable. 'What are APTs doing?' is not

The confidence level on the attribution is low - three TTPs overlap with known actor X, but the infrastructure has no clear overlap, and TTPs are shared between multiple groups. I reported it as 'consistent with' not 'attributed to'
We built a MISP event cluster for the campaign, linked the indicators to the STIX attack patterns, and shared it with the ISAC under TLP:GREEN
Pivoting on the registrar from the initial phishing domain found 14 related domains registered in the same 72-hour window, giving us proactive blocking before the campaign launched against us
red_flags: Cannot explain the Diamond Model or apply it to an example
Has never written a finished intelligence report
Thinks attribution means identifying the exact hacker's name
Cannot explain what PIR stands for or how to write one
Has never pivoted on infrastructure beyond a single IOC lookup
Cannot distinguish between a Cobalt Strike beacon and proprietary APT tooling from traffic patterns
Does not understand confidence levels or how to communicate analytic uncertainty
expected_tools: RiskIQ / PassiveTotal / SecurityTrails (passive DNS and historical infrastructure)
Recorded Future or similar TIP for enrichment
expected_frameworks: MITRE ATT&CK (technique mapping at analysis depth)
Diamond Model of Intrusion Analysis
Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain
Intelligence Cycle (Planning, Collection, Processing, Analysis, Dissemination, Feedback)
TLP (Traffic Light Protocol)
STIX 2.1 object model

senior.label: Senior CTI Analyst
characteristics: Produces strategic intelligence products consumed by executives and board-level leadership
Leads threat actor tracking programs: maintains dossiers on threat actors relevant to the organization, tracks campaigns over time
Has conducted intrusion attribution analysis with documented reasoning chains and confidence assessments
Applies structured analytic techniques: Analysis of Competing Hypotheses (ACH), Devil's Advocate analysis
Understands the difference between attribution and identification - and why conflating them is dangerous
Has contributed intelligence to ISACs or government partners (FS-ISAC, H-ISAC, CISA)
Designs intelligence collection plans with specific sources, collection methods, and gap analysis
Can evaluate a vendor threat intelligence report and identify what's analysis vs. what's speculation
Understands big four intelligence tradecraft: key assumptions check, alternative futures, indicators and warnings
Has experience with counter-intelligence: recognizing when an adversary is manipulating your intelligence collection
Integrates threat intelligence into detection engineering: converts finished intel into Sigma rules, ATT&CK layers, or EDR custom rules
Tracks threat actor evolution over time and identifies when TTPs shift - important for detection refresh
Has experience in a sector-specific context: financial sector, healthcare, energy, government
Can design and execute a threat intelligence-driven red team use case
Has built intelligence-sharing relationships with peer organizations, law enforcement, or government agencies
example_phrases: I use Analysis of Competing Hypotheses when attribution is ambiguous - list all hypotheses, identify the diagnostically useful evidence for each, and force yourself to explain what evidence would disprove your preferred hypothesis

The threat actor pivoted their infrastructure every 72 hours - traditional IOC sharing was useless. I changed our collection to focus on behavioral fingerprints: the custom HTTP header, the certificate subject fields, the specific JA3 hash combination

The vendor report said 'APT40 with high confidence' but cited three TTPs that are used by at least six different Chinese state-sponsored groups. I assessed it as 'likely Chinese state-sponsored' with the caveat that the 'APT40' attribution was not supported by the evidence presented

I presented the board with a geo-political risk assessment of nation-state targeting of our sector. The strategic intelligence product connected their motivation (disrupting financial infrastructure during political tensions) to specific operational patterns we'd observed over 18 months

We saw the actor shift from spearphishing to supply chain compromise after two of their spearphishing campaigns were publicly attributed. That TTP evolution told us our detection strategy needed to follow - I updated our ATT&CK coverage and triggered a supply chain security review
red_flags: Has never produced a strategic intelligence product
Cannot explain ACH (Analysis of Competing Hypotheses) or apply it
Has never contributed to an ISAC or external intelligence sharing
Attributes threat actors without articulating confidence levels or competing hypotheses
Has never integrated threat intelligence findings into detection content
Cannot evaluate a vendor's attribution claim critically
expected_tools: OpenCTI / MISP with custom object types
Network scanning and infrastructure analysis: Shodan API, Censys API, custom scripts
ATT&CK Navigator (custom layers for threat actor tracking)
expected_frameworks: Intelligence community tradecraft (structured analytic techniques)
Diamond Model (advanced application: activity threads, activity groups)
MITRE ATT&CK for Threat Intelligence (Group profiles, campaign tracking)
Kill Chain (targeting model and intelligence-driven defense)
STIX 2.1 / TAXII 2.1 (at developer/integration level)
TLP and PAP (Permissible Actions Protocol) for intelligence sharing

staff.label: Staff / CTI Program Lead
characteristics: Designs and leads the CTI program: requirements gathering, collection architecture, analyst workflow, product dissemination
Manages threat intelligence platform architecture and integration with SOC, IR, and detection engineering
Has government intelligence background or government partnership experience at the senior coordination level
Drives intelligence requirements from business stakeholders: translates business risk into intelligence collection priorities
Has published open-source threat research (blog posts, conference papers, public malware analysis)
Manages relationships with commercial threat intelligence vendors and evaluates their programs with rigor
Contributes to the development of the threat intelligence field: FIRST frameworks, ATT&CK contributions, CTI standards bodies
Has experience producing intelligence for M&A risk assessment, geopolitical risk, and strategic decision-making
Designs CTI metrics: intelligence actionability rate, indicator quality scoring, time from collection to operationalization
Has led joint investigations with law enforcement, government agencies, or IR retainer firms
Can build a threat-informed defense program: connecting CTI to red team prioritization, detection coverage, and IR playbook development
Understands the economics of threat intelligence: when to buy commercial intel vs. build in-house collection
Has experience communicating cyber threat intelligence to non-technical executives and board members
example_phrases: Our CTI program's primary customers are the detection team and the IR team - everything we produce has an explicit 'so what' and a recommended action. Reports nobody acts on aren't intelligence, they're research
I track our intelligence actionability rate: what percentage of finished products resulted in a detection update, an IR action, or a business decision. We target 70% and are at 65% currently

When evaluating commercial vendors, I ask for their collection methodology and how they handle source deconfliction. Most can't answer that. If they can't explain their sources, I can't assess their confidence

We designed the CTI program around our threat model, not around what threat actors are interesting. Ransomware operators targeting healthcare are boring to write about but they're what actually threatens us

I briefed the M&A team on cyber risk for three acquisition targets - the threat intelligence gave them leverage to negotiate a security remediation clause into one deal and kill another entirely based on active compromise indicators we found during due diligence
red_flags: Has no metrics for CTI program effectiveness
Cannot explain how intelligence should integrate with detection and IR teams
Has never evaluated a commercial threat intelligence vendor
Cannot explain how CTI supports a threat-informed defense program
Has no experience communicating CTI to executive or board-level audiences
expected_tools: Enterprise TIP: Recorded Future, Mandiant Advantage, MISP at admin level
Custom collection infrastructure: honeypots, domain monitoring, dark web monitoring
STIX/TAXII at integration and architecture level
Visualization tools: Maltego, custom graph databases (Neo4j)
expected_frameworks: Intelligence community standards (ICD 203, ICD 206 for analytic standards)
ATT&CK Threat Intelligence use case (threat group tracking, campaign analysis)
Diamond Model (program level: activity threads across campaigns over time)
CTI program maturity models
Threat-Informed Defense (MITRE Center for Threat-Informed Defense)
FIRST frameworks: TLP, CSIRT, Vulnerability Disclosure

positive: Distinguishes between what they know, what they assess, and what they don't know
Communicates confidence levels when making intelligence judgments
Immediately asks 'for whom' when asked about intelligence - considers the consumer
Explains what intelligence is actionable and what is just interesting
Uses 'consistent with' rather than 'attributed to' when evidence is incomplete
References specific threat groups by their actual tracked names (APT29, FIN7, TA543) not vague labels
Understands that TTPs are more durable intelligence than IOCs
Connects intelligence to specific defensive actions (detections, IR playbooks, network blocks)
negative: Describes threat intelligence exclusively as collecting and blocking IOCs
Uses 'Russia' or 'China' as an attribution answer without any evidence chain
Cannot distinguish between tactical, operational, and strategic intelligence
Does not understand TLP or why intelligence sharing requires access controls
Thinks the Diamond Model is about diamonds or gemstones (no conceptual understanding)
Cannot explain what the intelligence cycle is
Has never produced a finished intelligence product
Cannot articulate why IOC-based intelligence decays quickly
